picks.narrative: A 9.3pp gap: IBP has WSH at 66.4%, the market at 56.5%. The largest lift is pitcher quality (xfip) (~+1.8pp): Foster Griffin 3.44 xFIP vs Jacob Lopez 5.61 xFIP. The main pushback is home field (~-0.7pp): OAK's home-field context weighs against the pick playing on the road. The price has held roughly steady since posting — the current -133 still sits inside the playable range (to -166). The win condition is five-plus quality innings from Foster Griffin and avoiding early bullpen exposure.

picks.narrative: Model and market disagree on LAD by 8.0pp — 60.3% fair against a 52.8% price. Several smaller signals stack the same way — lineup quality (~+0.7pp), defense (oaa) (~+0.6pp) — with no single one dominating. The market has drifted against the pick since posting, widening the available price — the current -110 still sits inside the playable range (to -129). The case is a stack of small same-direction signals, not one dominant mismatch — treat it as the thinner edge it is.

picks.narrative: The model prices SF at 44.3% to win where the no-vig market implies 38.1%, a 6.9pp disagreement. There's no standout starter, lineup, or bullpen signal driving this — the gap comes from the model's baseline read of the side, not a visible matchup edge. The main pushback is pitcher quality (xfip) (~-1.5pp): Robbie Ray 4.84 xFIP vs Logan Gilbert 3.27 xFIP. With a headwind that size, this is not a clean all-signal read. The market has drifted against the pick since posting, widening the available price — the current +167 still sits inside the playable range (to +148). This is mostly a price play — the model likes the number more than the matchup, so the baseball case is mixed.

The model isn't leaning on a marquee matchup here — no single baseball factor rises to a headline; the disagreement is with the posted price itself. The market has drifted against the pick since posting, widening the available price — the current -109 still sits inside the playable range (to -119). The read here is the price, not the profile — the model just makes this a better side than the posted number does.

picks.narrative: IBP values STL 6.0pp higher than the market — 53.7% model vs 47.4% implied. No individual baseball factor clears the bar for a headline here — this is not a single visible mismatch, it is the model pricing the side differently than the market. The main pushback is defense (oaa) (~-0.6pp): ARI defense grades +16 OAA better on the season — a run-prevention headwind. The price has held roughly steady since posting — the current +110 still sits inside the playable range (to +101). This isn't a clean all-signal agreement — it's the model calling the posted number too generous for the true win probability.

picks.narrative: The model sees PIT as a 59.0% side; the market is pricing 53.3%, leaving a 5.5pp edge. The largest lift is pitcher quality (xfip) (~+0.7pp): Paul Skenes 3.12 xFIP vs Joey Cantillo 3.86 xFIP. The main pushback is home field (~-1.1pp): CLE's home-field context weighs against the pick playing on the road. With a headwind that size, this is not a clean all-signal read. The price has held roughly steady since posting — the current -115 still sits inside the playable range (to -122). The edge is the model pricing PIT differently than the market rather than one of the marquee starter/lineup/bullpen buckets — worth taking at the number, not chasing past it.

picks.narrative: Model and market disagree on COL by 4.5pp — 43.9% fair against a 39.5% price. The largest lift is home field (~+1.1pp): Home-park and 2026 home-game context add a modest edge for COL. The main pushback is pitcher quality (xfip) (~-2.9pp): Ryan Feltner 5.10 xFIP vs Hunter Greene 1.98 xFIP. With a headwind that size, this is not a clean all-signal read. The price has held roughly steady since posting — the current +154 still sits inside the playable range (to +151). This is value in the number more than the matchup — the model just makes COL a better side than the price does.
